answer:
Designing a Practical API Gateway for a Mid-Size Product

1. Definition & Context

An API Gateway acts as the single entry-point for all client requests, handling cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, request routing, and response aggregation. For a mid-size product, the gateway must balance simplicity with extensibility — avoiding the overhead of a full service mesh while still decoupling front-end clients from back-end microservices.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System at VisionQuest Solutions, the Angular 18 SPA communicated with multiple Fastify microservices — leave service, notification service, and approval-chain service. Rather than exposing three origins, Manoj designed a lightweight API Gateway using Fastify with fastify-http-proxy that sat in front of all services on a single domain.

The gateway handled JWT validation (via a preHandler hook), attached x-user-id and x-role headers to downstream requests, and used prefix-based routing: /api/leave/* → leave service, /api/notify/* → notification service, /api/approval/* → approval service. It also aggregated health checks from all three services into a single /health endpoint that the Render deployment monitored.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// gateway.ts — Fastify API Gateway
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import proxy from '@fastify/http-proxy';
import jwt from '@fastify/jwt';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });

// Register JWT verification
app.register(jwt, { secret: process.env.JWT_SECRET! });

// Auth preHandler — runs before every proxied request
app.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
  if (req.url.startsWith('/api/health')) return; // skip health
  try {
    const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
    req.headers['x-user-id'] = decoded.sub;
    req.headers['x-role'] = decoded.role;
  } catch {
    reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Invalid token' });
  }
});

// Proxy routes to downstream services
app.register(proxy, {
  upstream: process.env.LEAVE_SERVICE_URL!,
  prefix: '/api/leave',
  rewritePrefix: '/leave',
});

app.register(proxy, {
  upstream: process.env.APPROVAL_SERVICE_URL!,
  prefix: '/api/approval',
  rewritePrefix: '/approval',
});

app.register(proxy, {
  upstream: process.env.NOTIFY_SERVICE_URL!,
  prefix: '/api/notify',
  rewritePrefix: '/notify',
});

// Aggregated health check
app.get('/api/health', async () => {
  const checks = await Promise.allSettled([
    fetch(process.env.LEAVE_SERVICE_URL + '/health'),
    fetch(process.env.APPROVAL_SERVICE_URL + '/health'),
    fetch(process.env.NOTIFY_SERVICE_URL + '/health'),
  ]);
  return {
    gateway: 'ok',
    services: checks.map((c, i) =>
      c.status === 'fulfilled' ? 'ok' : 'down'
    ),
  };
});

app.listen({ port: 3000 });

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During HR Leave System testing, Manoj noticed that POST /api/leave/apply returned 413 Payload Too Large intermittently. The leave-apply endpoint accepted file attachments (medical certificates), and the gateway's default body size limit was 1 MB. He diagnosed it by:

  1. Checking Fastify gateway logs — saw FST_ERR_CTP_BODY_TOO_LARGE at the proxy layer, confirming the gateway rejected the request before it reached the leave service.
  2. Adding bodyLimit: 10 * 1024 * 1024 to the proxy registration for /api/leave only, keeping other routes at 1 MB for security.
  3. Also discovered the proxy wasn't forwarding content-type: multipart/form-data correctly — fixed by setting contentTypesToEncode: [] so the proxy passed the raw body stream.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Gateway becomes a single point of failureDeploy two gateway replicas behind Render's load balancer with sticky sessions disabled; add circuit breakers per upstream using @fastify/circuit-breaker
Latency added by an extra network hopKeep gateway and services in the same Render region; use connection keep-alive; measure p99 latency with custom Fastify hooks
JWT secret rotation breaks all services simultaneouslyUse asymmetric RS256 keys — gateway verifies with public key, auth service signs with private key; rotate without downtime

7. Validation Strategy

  • Integration test: Spin up gateway + mock upstreams with nock; verify routing, auth rejection, and header propagation
  • Load test: Use autocannon to verify gateway handles 500 RPS with p99 < 50ms to downstream stubs
  • Health endpoint test: Kill one mock upstream, verify /api/health reports partial degradation (not 500)
  • Playwright E2E: Full flow — login → apply leave → verify gateway correctly proxies and returns 201

Debugging a Real API Gateway Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging an API Gateway issue requires systematic reasoning about which layer failed — the client, the gateway itself, or the downstream service. In an interview, you demonstrate this by walking through a real incident with clear diagnostic steps, showing you understand network boundaries, header propagation, timeout cascades, and observability.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a production incident occurred where managers could not approve leave requests after a Friday deployment. The Angular SPA showed a generic "Request Failed" toast. The bug was in the API Gateway layer — a recent change to JWT validation logic accidentally required a department claim that only existed in employee tokens, not manager tokens (managers had a managedDepartments array instead).

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — broken gateway auth hook
app.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
  const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
  // BUG: managers don't have 'department', they have 'managedDepartments'
  if (!decoded.department) {
    reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Missing department claim' });
    return;
  }
  req.headers['x-department'] = decoded.department;
});

// AFTER — fixed to handle both token shapes
app.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
  const decoded = await req.jwtVerify();
  const dept = decoded.department
    || (decoded.managedDepartments?.[0]); // manager's primary dept
  if (!dept) {
    req.log.warn({ sub: decoded.sub, role: decoded.role },
      'Token missing department info');
    reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Insufficient claims' });
    return;
  }
  req.headers['x-department'] = dept;
  req.headers['x-role'] = decoded.role;
});

// Test to prevent regression
import { test } from 'node:test';
import assert from 'node:assert';

test('gateway accepts manager tokens', async () => {
  const managerToken = app.jwt.sign({
    sub: 'mgr-42',
    role: 'manager',
    managedDepartments: ['engineering', 'design'],
  });
  const res = await app.inject({
    method: 'GET',
    url: '/api/leave/pending',
    headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${managerToken}` },
  });
  assert.notStrictEqual(res.statusCode, 401,
    'Manager token should not be rejected');
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj walked through the incident timeline during a mock interview:

  1. Detection: Slack alert from the manager team at 9:30 AM Monday — "can't approve any leaves since Friday."
  2. Triage: Opened browser DevTools on manager's machine — all PATCH /api/approval/:id requests returned 401, but employee leave-apply (POST) worked fine.
  3. Hypothesis: Auth is broken for manager role specifically. Decoded the manager's JWT on jwt.io — no department field, but had managedDepartments: ["hr", "finance"].
  4. Root cause: git log --oneline -5 on gateway repo showed Friday's commit "add department header propagation" — the new code assumed all tokens had department.
  5. Fix: 4-line change + regression test. Deployed hotfix in 20 minutes. Added a pre-deploy checklist item: "test with all role types."

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Assuming all JWT tokens have identical claim shapesDefine a TypeScript discriminated union for token types: EmployeeToken | ManagerToken | AdminToken with exhaustive checks
No role-specific test coverage in CIAdd parameterized tests that inject tokens for each role — employee, manager, admin, HR — and verify gateway passes them through
Friday deploys with no weekend monitoringImplement deploy freeze on Fridays or require canary deployment with automatic rollback if error rate > 1%

7. Validation Strategy

  • Role matrix test: Automated test matrix covering employee, manager, admin, and HR tokens against every gateway route
  • Contract test: Verify JWT claim schema between auth service and gateway using shared TypeScript interface
  • Canary deploy: Roll out gateway changes to 10% traffic first, monitor 401 rate for 15 minutes before full rollout
  • Alerting: Set up PagerDuty alert when 401 rate exceeds 5% in a 5-minute window (would have caught this in under 10 minutes)

Designing a Practical BFF (Backend for Frontend) Pattern Implementation

1. Definition & Context

The Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern creates a dedicated backend layer tailored to a specific frontend's needs. Instead of a generic API that serves web, mobile, and third-party clients with the same endpoints, each client type gets its own BFF that aggregates, transforms, and optimizes data specifically for that UI. This reduces over-fetching, simplifies frontend logic, and allows independent evolution of each client's API contract.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard at VisionQuest, the Angular 18 SPA needed data from three backend domains — leads, deals, and activity logs — to render the dashboard overview. The raw Express.js APIs returned full entity objects (50+ fields each), but the dashboard cards only needed 5-6 fields per entity. Mobile sales reps needed a completely different shape — a flat list of today's activities with inline lead names.

Manoj proposed a Web BFF layer in Express.js that sat between the Angular SPA and the domain APIs. The BFF exposed three endpoints: GET /bff/dashboard-summary (aggregated 3 API calls into one response with only the fields the dashboard needed), GET /bff/pipeline-chart (pre-computed stage-by-stage deal counts), and GET /bff/recent-activities (joined activity logs with lead names server-side). This cut the number of Angular HTTP calls from 6 to 3 and reduced payload size by 70%.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// web-bff/routes/dashboard.ts — Express.js Web BFF
import { Router } from 'express';
import axios from 'axios';

const router = Router();
const LEADS_URL = process.env.LEADS_API!;
const DEALS_URL = process.env.DEALS_API!;
const ACTIVITY_URL = process.env.ACTIVITY_API!;

// Aggregated dashboard summary — single call replaces 3 frontend calls
router.get('/dashboard-summary', async (req, res) => {
  const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'] as string;

  const [leads, deals, activities] = await Promise.all([
    axios.get(`${LEADS_URL}/leads`, {
      params: { assignee: userId, status: 'open' },
      headers: { 'x-user-id': userId },
    }),
    axios.get(`${DEALS_URL}/deals`, {
      params: { owner: userId, fields: 'id,title,stage,value' },
    }),
    axios.get(`${ACTIVITY_URL}/activities`, {
      params: { userId, limit: 10, sort: '-createdAt' },
    }),
  ]);

  // Shape response specifically for dashboard UI cards
  res.json({
    openLeads: leads.data.length,
    leadsByStage: groupBy(leads.data, 'stage'),
    pipelineValue: deals.data.reduce(
      (sum: number, d: any) => sum + d.value, 0
    ),
    dealsByStage: groupBy(deals.data, 'stage'),
    recentActivities: activities.data.map((a: any) => ({
      id: a.id,
      type: a.type,
      summary: a.summary,
      createdAt: a.createdAt,
    })),
  });
});

function groupBy(arr: T[], key: keyof T): Record {
  return arr.reduce((acc: any, item: any) => {
    const k = item[key];
    acc[k] = (acc[k] || 0) + 1;
    return acc;
  }, {});
}

export default router;

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

The CRM dashboard BFF worked well until the sales team grew from 5 to 30 reps. The /bff/dashboard-summary endpoint started timing out (>5s) because Promise.all fanned out to three APIs, and the Leads API was slow for users with 200+ open leads.

Manoj debugged it by adding console.time labels around each API call to identify the bottleneck — Leads API was taking 3.8s alone. The fix was two-fold: (1) added limit: 50 and pagination to the Leads API call since the dashboard only showed a count and top 10, and (2) added a 30-second Redis cache (ioredis) for the lead count, since the exact count didn't need real-time accuracy for dashboard cards. Response time dropped from 5s to 400ms.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
BFF becomes a "fat" layer with business logic that belongs in domain servicesStrict rule: BFF only aggregates, reshapes, and caches — zero business rules. Code review checklist item: "Does this BFF route contain a business decision?"
Code duplication between Web BFF and Mobile BFFExtract shared data-fetching utilities into a shared api-client package; each BFF only handles response shaping
N+1 API calls when BFF joins data across servicesAdd batch endpoints to domain APIs (POST /leads/batch) and use DataLoader pattern in BFF to batch concurrent lookups

7. Validation Strategy

  • Contract test: Verify BFF response shape matches Angular service interface using zod schema validation in integration tests
  • Performance benchmark: BFF must respond in < 500ms for dashboard-summary with mock downstream latency of 100ms each
  • Failure isolation: If one downstream API is down, BFF returns partial data with a degraded: true flag instead of 500
  • Angular integration: Playwright test verifying dashboard renders correctly when BFF returns expected shape

Debugging a Real BFF Pattern Issue in an Interview Context

1. Definition & Context

Debugging BFF issues requires understanding the aggregation boundaries — when a BFF combines multiple downstream API responses into one, errors can come from any downstream service, the BFF's transformation logic, or the network layer between them. The key interview skill is demonstrating that you can isolate which layer is faulty using structured reasoning and observability tools.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the sales team reported that the pipeline chart showed incorrect deal counts — the chart displayed 45 deals in "Negotiation" stage, but filtering the deals list showed only 12. The bug was in the Web BFF's /bff/pipeline-chart endpoint, which was counting deals across ALL users instead of filtering by the logged-in sales rep's x-user-id header.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — BFF pipeline-chart (bug: missing owner filter)
router.get('/pipeline-chart', async (req, res) => {
  // BUG: no userId filter — returns ALL deals across ALL reps
  const { data } = await axios.get(`${DEALS_URL}/deals`, {
    params: { fields: 'stage' },
  });
  const stages = groupBy(data, 'stage');
  res.json({ stages });
});

// AFTER — Fixed with user scoping
router.get('/pipeline-chart', async (req, res) => {
  const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'] as string;
  if (!userId) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Missing user context' });
  }
  const { data } = await axios.get(`${DEALS_URL}/deals`, {
    params: { owner: userId, fields: 'stage' },
  });
  const stages = groupBy(data, 'stage');
  res.json({ stages, userId }); // include userId for debugging
});

// Regression test
describe('GET /bff/pipeline-chart', () => {
  it('scopes deals to the authenticated user', async () => {
    // Mock Deals API to track received params
    nock(DEALS_URL)
      .get('/deals')
      .query((q: any) => q.owner === 'user-77')
      .reply(200, [
        { stage: 'Negotiation' },
        { stage: 'Closed Won' },
      ]);

    const res = await request(app)
      .get('/bff/pipeline-chart')
      .set('x-user-id', 'user-77');

    expect(res.body.stages).toEqual({
      Negotiation: 1,
      'Closed Won': 1,
    });
    expect(res.body.userId).toBe('user-77');
  });

  it('returns 401 when x-user-id header is missing', async () => {
    const res = await request(app).get('/bff/pipeline-chart');
    expect(res.status).toBe(401);
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj detected this bug during a demo to the sales manager. The diagnosis took 15 minutes:

  1. Observation: Pipeline chart showed company-wide totals, but the deals table (which called the Deals API directly with userId) showed correct per-user counts.
  2. Comparison: Used Postman to call GET /bff/pipeline-chart with and without x-user-id header — same result both times, confirming the BFF ignored the header.
  3. Code review: Found the /pipeline-chart route was copy-pasted from an admin dashboard route (which intentionally showed all deals) and the userId filter was never added.
  4. Prevention: Added a shared middleware requireUserId that all non-admin BFF routes use, so missing user context is caught at the middleware level rather than per-route.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Copy-paste routes without adapting filtersUse route-generator functions that enforce required parameters — createScopedRoute(handler, { requireUserId: true })
BFF returns data for wrong user (security issue)Add integration tests that verify data isolation — call BFF with user A's token and assert zero results from user B's data
No visibility into what params BFF sends downstreamLog outgoing API calls with params at DEBUG level; use correlation IDs to trace request chains

7. Validation Strategy

  • Data isolation test: Seed test DB with deals for 3 users; verify each user's BFF response contains only their deals
  • Middleware coverage: Verify all non-admin BFF routes use requireUserId middleware via a route registration audit test
  • Postman collection: Shared collection with pre-request scripts that set x-user-id from environment, preventing accidental unscoped calls
  • Code review rule: Any new BFF route must include a "scoping" section in the PR description explaining how user context is applied

Comparing Two Caching Strategies with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Caching stores frequently accessed data closer to the consumer to reduce latency and backend load. The two primary strategies are Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading) — the application checks the cache first, fetches from the DB on miss, and populates the cache — and Write-Through — every write updates both the cache and the DB synchronously, ensuring the cache is always fresh. Choosing between them depends on read/write ratio, consistency requirements, and acceptable staleness.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, two different data patterns required different caching strategies:

Cache-Aside was used for the employee directory lookup — when rendering the leave approval chain, the system needed manager names and email addresses. This data changed rarely (only on org changes), so on each leave request, the system checked Redis first, fetched from PostgreSQL on miss, and cached for 1 hour. This reduced DB queries by 85% during peak leave-application periods (month-end).

Write-Through was used for leave balance tracking. When HR approved a leave, the system simultaneously updated the PostgreSQL leave_balances table AND the Redis cache. This ensured that when employees checked their remaining balance (a very frequent read), the cache was always accurate — critical because showing stale balance could lead to over-applying for leaves.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Cache-Aside pattern — employee directory lookup
import Redis from 'ioredis';
import { prisma } from './db';

const redis = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);

async function getEmployee(id: string) {
  const cacheKey = `emp:${id}`;

  // 1. Check cache
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  // 2. Cache miss — fetch from DB
  const employee = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
    where: { id },
    select: { id: true, name: true, email: true, department: true },
  });
  if (!employee) return null;

  // 3. Populate cache with 1-hour TTL
  await redis.setex(cacheKey, 3600, JSON.stringify(employee));
  return employee;
}

// Write-Through pattern — leave balance update
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, days: number) {
  // 1. Update DB (source of truth)
  const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.update({
    where: { id: leaveId },
    data: { status: 'approved' },
    include: { employee: true },
  });

  // 2. Update balance in DB
  const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.update({
    where: { employeeId: leave.employeeId },
    data: { remaining: { decrement: days } },
  });

  // 3. Write-Through: update cache immediately
  const balanceKey = `balance:${leave.employeeId}`;
  await redis.setex(balanceKey, 86400, JSON.stringify(balance));

  return { leave, balance };
}

// Read balance — always hits cache (populated by write-through)
async function getLeaveBalance(employeeId: string) {
  const cached = await redis.get(`balance:${employeeId}`);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  // Fallback: cold start or cache eviction
  const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({
    where: { employeeId },
  });
  if (balance) {
    await redis.setex(`balance:${employeeId}`, 86400, JSON.stringify(balance));
  }
  return balance;
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj encountered a subtle bug in the HR Leave System where employees saw stale leave balances after their manager approved a leave. The write-through logic updated balance:${employeeId} correctly, but a separate endpoint — GET /api/leave/summary — had its own Cache-Aside cache with key summary:${employeeId} that included the balance. This second cache had a 30-minute TTL and wasn't invalidated by the write-through.

Diagnosis: Used redis-cli MONITOR to watch cache operations during an approval flow — saw SET balance:emp-5 but no DEL summary:emp-5. Fix: Added explicit invalidation of related cache keys when balance changed, using a invalidateRelated(employeeId) function that deleted all keys matching *:${employeeId}.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Multiple cache keys for the same entity getting out of syncUse a cache-key registry — when entity X changes, look up all cache keys that include X and invalidate them atomically with redis.pipeline()
Write-through adds latency to every write operationUse write-behind (async) for non-critical caches — queue the cache update and let a background worker handle it within 1 second
Cache-aside TTL too long causes stale reads during business-critical periodsUse event-driven invalidation — publish a balance.updated event that triggers cache eviction, regardless of TTL

7. Validation Strategy

  • Consistency test: Approve a leave, immediately read balance via API — assert cached value matches DB value
  • Staleness test: For cache-aside, verify TTL is respected — set TTL to 1s in test, wait 2s, verify cache miss triggers DB fetch
  • Race condition test: Two concurrent approvals for same employee — verify final balance is correct (use Prisma's decrement for atomicity)
  • Cache failure resilience: Kill Redis in test, verify app falls back to DB-only mode without errors

Designing a Practical Caching Strategy Implementation

1. Definition & Context

A practical caching strategy for a mid-size product must address what to cache (hot paths only), where to cache (in-memory, Redis, CDN), when to invalidate (TTL, event-driven, or manual), and how to handle failures (graceful degradation). The goal is to reduce database load and API latency while maintaining acceptable data freshness for the specific use case.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub personal project, the contact list page was the most visited screen. Each visit triggered a MongoDB query with sorting, pagination, and search filtering. With 500+ contacts after CSV bulk uploads, the query took 200-400ms. Manoj implemented a multi-layer caching strategy:

  • Layer 1 — React Query client cache: Cached the last-fetched page in memory with a 30-second staleTime, so navigating away and back didn't re-fetch.
  • Layer 2 — Fastify response cache: Used @fastify/caching with ETag support. Subsequent requests with matching ETag got 304 Not Modified (zero body transfer).
  • Layer 3 — MongoDB query cache: Created a compound index on { userId: 1, name: 1 } so the most common sort-by-name query used a covered index (no collection scan).

3. Multi-Layer Cache Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Layer 1 — React Query cache (ContactsHub frontend)
import { useQuery } from '@tanstack/react-query';

function useContacts(page: number, search: string) {
  return useQuery({
    queryKey: ['contacts', page, search],
    queryFn: () =>
      fetch(`/api/contacts?page=${page}&search=${search}`, {
        headers: { 'If-None-Match': getStoredETag('contacts') },
      }).then(async (res) => {
        if (res.status === 304) return getCachedData('contacts');
        const etag = res.headers.get('etag');
        if (etag) storeETag('contacts', etag);
        const data = await res.json();
        storeCachedData('contacts', data);
        return data;
      }),
    staleTime: 30_000, // 30 seconds
    gcTime: 5 * 60_000, // keep in memory 5 min
  });
}

// Layer 2 — Fastify ETag caching (ContactsHub backend)
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';
import crypto from 'crypto';

async function contactRoutes(app: FastifyInstance) {
  app.get('/api/contacts', async (req, reply) => {
    const { page = '1', search = '' } = req.query as any;
    const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'];

    const contacts = await Contact.find({
      userId,
      name: { $regex: search, $options: 'i' },
    })
      .sort({ name: 1 })
      .skip((+page - 1) * 20)
      .limit(20)
      .lean();

    // Generate ETag from content hash
    const etag = crypto
      .createHash('md5')
      .update(JSON.stringify(contacts))
      .digest('hex');

    // Return 304 if client has current version
    if (req.headers['if-none-match'] === etag) {
      reply.code(304).send();
      return;
    }

    reply.header('etag', etag).send(contacts);
  });
}

// Layer 3 — MongoDB compound index
// In Mongoose schema setup:
contactSchema.index({ userId: 1, name: 1 }); // covered index for sort-by-name

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying the ETag caching in ContactsHub, Manoj noticed that editing a contact didn't reflect on the list immediately — the user had to wait 30 seconds (React Query staleTime) or refresh manually. The ETag was working correctly (new data had a new hash), but React Query was serving stale data from its L1 cache without even making a network request.

Fix: After a successful contact update mutation, Manoj added queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['contacts'] }) to force React Query to refetch immediately. He also added an onSuccess callback to the mutation that called queryClient.setQueryData for optimistic updates — the UI updated instantly while the background refetch confirmed the server state.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
ETag generation hashes the full response on every request — expensive for large datasetsUse a version counter in the database; increment on any write; use the counter as the ETag value instead of hashing
React Query cache and server cache can diverge after mutationsAlways invalidate React Query cache on mutation success; use optimistic updates for instant UI feedback
Compound index bloats MongoDB memory for large collectionsOnly index fields actually used in queries; use explain() to verify index usage; set index hints in Mongoose queries

7. Validation Strategy

  • Cache hit rate: Log L1/L2/L3 hit/miss counts; target 80%+ cache hit rate for contact list page
  • Freshness test: Update contact, navigate to list — verify update visible within 1 second (optimistic update) and confirmed within 5 seconds (background refetch)
  • 304 verification: Playwright test that loads contacts, navigates away, returns — verify Network tab shows 304 (not 200 with full body)
  • Index coverage: Run db.contacts.find(...).explain('executionStats') and assert totalDocsExamined === totalKeysExamined (covered query)
